title: Existence and Regularity of Nonlocal Minimal Surfaces
speaker: Michele Caselli
abstract: In the '80s, Yau conjectured that every closed Riemannian 3-manifold contains infinitely many smooth minimal hypersurfaces. In this talk, I will present a Yau-type existence result for nonlocal minimal surfaces and discuss how one can recover the classical Yau's conjecture from it. The main tools are min-max methods for the fractional perimeter and a compactness/regularity theory for the associated critical points, which gives smoothness in low dimensions and a small singular set in higher dimensions. Time permitting, I will discuss a broader program about extending these ideas to higher codimension.

proof_stages.label: Classical Yau problem and compactness obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the problem of constructing infinitely many smooth embedded minimal hypersurfaces, explains why such families must have unbounded index and usually arise by min-max, and identifies multiplicity/noncompactness of classical minimal surfaces as the main obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Fractional perimeter variational framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the fractional perimeter, its convergence to classical perimeter, s-minimality, stability and weak index, and recasts codimension-one s-minimal surfaces as fractional Sobolev maps into S^0.

proof_stages.label: Min-max existence of fractional critical hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: Using fractional Allen-Cahn/min-max methods, one obtains for each p an s-minimal hypersurface with controlled index and the expected width growth, and after the analytic estimates the resulting fractional critical sets have distinct fractional perimeters without a multiplicity-one step.

proof_stages.label: Uniform BV compactness for bounded index
proof_stages.summary: The central analytic theorem gives epsilon-independent BV/perimeter bounds for bounded-index solutions of the fractional Allen-Cahn equation or bounded-index s-minimal surfaces, yielding strong compactness and preventing energy loss at fixed s.

proof_stages.label: Lambda-stability and covering proof of the BV estimate
proof_stages.summary: The proof starts from stable BV estimates, weakens stability to lambda-stability to handle nonlocal interactions between disjoint supports, and converts finite index into local estimates through a combinatorial covering theorem for sufficiently separated balls.

proof_stages.label: Finite-index regularity and Liouville theorem
proof_stages.summary: The BV estimate, blow-up/blow-down compactness, stability inequalities, cone classification and improvement of flatness imply that in dimensions three and four smooth finite-index s-minimal hypersurfaces in Euclidean space must be flat.

proof_stages.label: Limit as s approaches one and classical minimal surfaces
proof_stages.summary: Known results are used to explain how stable or finite-index s-minimal hypersurfaces recover classical minimal surfaces as s tends to one, while the subsequent discussion clarifies that multiplicity and exceptional convergence phenomena may still occur in this limiting process.

proof_stages.label: Codimension-two fractional mass via currents
proof_stages.summary: The final part sketches an oriented codimension-two analogue: define a fractional mass of an integral current by minimizing fractional S^1-valued Sobolev energy with prescribed Jacobian, prove gamma-convergence and compactness toward classical mass, and discuss the open regularity/existence program in higher codimension.

examples.name: Bumpy positive-scalar-curvature three-manifold with finitely many bounded-index minimal surfaces
examples.note: The Chodosh-Maximo finiteness result is cited to illustrate that any infinite family of classical minimal surfaces must have unbounded Morse index, hence should be sought by min-max methods.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites the bumpy positive-scalar-curvature three-manifold result as a known example showing only finitely many bounded-index minimal surfaces, without working it out

It is used as an obstruction to finding infinitely many minimal surfaces with bounded index in a generic bumpy PSC setting.

used as an obstruction showing bounded-index minimal surfaces cannot supply infinitely many examples in such manifolds

examples.name: Many parallel hyperplanes in a ball
examples.note: The local perimeter example shows why a uniform BV/perimeter bound cannot hold for stable classical minimal hypersurfaces, thereby highlighting the nonlocal layer-repulsion mechanism in the fractional theory.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses arbitrarily many hyperplanes in a ball as a concrete counterexample, reasoning that their perimeter can be made arbitrarily large while remaining stable/local minimizing

passes.reason: Flat parallel sheets are trivial highly regular minimal hypersurfaces used to refute a uniform classical BV bound.

flat parallel hyperplanes are area-minimizing symmetric sheets used to disprove the analogous classical BV bound
